h1Title: Trigeminal Neuralgia Surgery: MVD, Radiosurgery & Outcomes
heroOverview: Trigeminal neuralgia (TN) produces the most severe facial pain known to medicine — sudden, electric-shock-like volleys lasting under two seconds that are triggered by ordinary actions such as chewing, speaking, or touching the face. It affects 4-5 per 100,000 people annually, with women and adults over 50 disproportionately affected. The underlying mechanism in most cases is pulsatile compression of the trigeminal nerve root at the brainstem by an aberrant artery or vein, causing focal demyelination that generates ectopic electrical discharges. When carbamazepine, the first-line drug, fails to control pain or causes intolerable side effects, three main surgical options achieve durable relief: microvascular decompression (MVD), stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife), and percutaneous ablative procedures.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence approximately 4-5 per 100,000 persons per year; lifetime prevalence roughly 0.3% of the general population
keyStats.demographics: Women affected roughly 1.5-2× more often than men; predominantly a disease of adults over 50; younger onset should prompt MRI to exclude multiple sclerosis or posterior fossa tumour
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis approximately 53-57 years; rare under age 40 except in multiple sclerosis
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1-2 million people worldwide living with trigeminal neuralgia; significant under-diagnosis estimated at 50% globally

definition: Trigeminal neuralgia (ICD-10: G50.0) is a chronic pain condition of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V) characterised by unilateral, sudden, severe, brief (typically under 2 seconds), and repetitive electric-shock-like facial pain episodes triggered by light tactile stimuli to the face, lips, gums, teeth, or oral cavity. It is classified as Classical TN when caused by neurovascular compression at the trigeminal root entry zone, Secondary TN when due to structural lesions (multiple sclerosis plaques, tumours, vascular malformations), and Idiopathic TN when no cause is identified on imaging. Pain is distributed in the territory of the maxillary (V2) and/or mandibular (V3) divisions in approximately 70% of cases; ophthalmic (V1) involvement is present in under 10% of cases. The surgical modalities — microvascular decompression via posterior fossa craniotomy, percutaneous ablative procedures via the foramen ovale, and stereotactic radiosurgery — are performed by neurosurgeons and are indicated when pharmacological treatment with carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine fails, when side effects are intolerable, or when the pain severity markedly impairs quality of life. TN has a significant impact on nutrition, sleep, and mental health, with depression rates approaching 40% in poorly controlled cases.

typesClassification.description: Episodes of purely paroxysmal electric-shock pain with complete pain-free intervals between attacks. Most commonly caused by superior cerebellar artery compression of the trigeminal root. MVD has the best outcomes in this type.

typesClassification.type: Classical TN with concomitant continuous pain (Type 2)
typesClassification.description: Paroxysmal attacks superimposed on a background of continuous or near-continuous aching or burning facial pain. This mixed phenotype has a less favourable surgical outcome than pure paroxysmal TN.

TN resulting from an identifiable structural cause: multiple sclerosis plaques at the root entry zone (4-5% of TN), posterior fossa tumours (meningioma, epidermoid cyst), or vascular malformations. Secondary TN requires treatment of the underlying cause in addition to pain surgery.

Clinically typical TN without detectable neurovascular compression on high-resolution MRI. Surgery carries a lower success rate; percutaneous ablative procedures or radiosurgery are often preferred over craniotomy.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, severe, unilateral facial pain described as electric shock, stabbing, or lightning bolt in quality, lasting a fraction of a second to two seconds per volley, often occurring in rapid repetitive bursts.
Pain distributed strictly in trigeminal nerve territory — typically cheek, teeth, gum, jaw, or lips (V2/V3 distribution); less commonly forehead and eye (V1).
Trigger zones activated by light tactile contact: eating, speaking, brushing teeth, swallowing, a light touch on the nasolabial fold, or exposure to wind reliably provoke attacks.
Complete absence of facial pain between paroxysms in the classical type — patients are entirely pain-free for minutes to days between clusters of attacks.
Wincing, involuntary facial grimacing, or catching the breath during an attack ('tic douloureux' — the painful spasm).
Avoidance behaviours: patients stop eating, refuse to speak, cannot brush teeth, and avoid cold air to minimise trigger activation; severe cases lead to weight loss and malnutrition.
Unilateral distribution — bilateral TN occurs in less than 3% of cases and strongly suggests multiple sclerosis.
No objective sensory loss on examination in classical type 1 TN — the presence of facial numbness suggests secondary TN or prior ablative surgery.
Refractory periods after a series of attacks when the nerve temporarily cannot be re-triggered, lasting seconds to minutes.
Anxiety and anticipatory dread around mealtimes, social interactions, and dental appointments, secondary to pain unpredictability.
earlyWarningSigns: Brief electric-shock facial pain initially dismissed as toothache, leading to unnecessary dental extractions without pain relief — a classic diagnostic delay in TN.
Onset of trigger sensitivity around the nasolabial fold or lips that makes eating or speaking progressively difficult over weeks.
Multiple failed dental procedures (root canals, extractions) without pain resolution in a patient over 50 with unilateral facial pain.
First episode of TN in a patient under 45, especially with other neurological symptoms — requires urgent MRI to exclude multiple sclerosis.
Pain frequency increasing despite adequate carbamazepine dose, signalling pharmacological tolerance or disease progression.
emergencySigns: Continuous severe facial pain without pain-free intervals (status trigeminalis) — intractable pain over 24 hours may require inpatient IV phenytoin or lidocaine while awaiting definitive surgical planning.
New facial numbness, weakness of jaw closure, or corneal reflex loss accompanying facial pain — suggests secondary TN from a posterior fossa mass requiring urgent MRI.
Facial pain with diplopia, hearing loss, or cerebellar signs — signs of posterior fossa lesion (tumour or vascular malformation) that requires immediate imaging.
Severe weight loss or dehydration from inability to eat or swallow in a patient with medically refractory TN — hospital admission for nutritional support and urgent surgical planning.

causes.cause: Neurovascular compression at the trigeminal root entry zone
causes.description: An aberrant or tortuous artery — most commonly the superior cerebellar artery in 75% of cases, followed by the anterior inferior cerebellar artery and veins — compresses the trigeminal nerve where it enters the brainstem. The pulsatile compression causes focal demyelination, allowing cross-talk between touch and pain fibres that underlies the characteristic touch-triggered pain. This mechanism is confirmed at surgery in over 80% of classical TN cases.

causes.cause: Multiple sclerosis plaque at the root entry zone
causes.description: MS plaques in the pons at the trigeminal entry zone disrupt myelination and produce ectopic firing identical to classical TN. MS-related TN is often bilateral, begins at younger ages, and may remit with MS relapses and remissions. The prevalence of TN in MS patients is approximately 2%, far higher than the general population.

Meningiomas, epidermoid cysts, acoustic neuromas, and vascular malformations in the posterior fossa can compress or distort the trigeminal nerve, producing secondary TN. These must be excluded by MRI before attributing pain to classical neurovascular compression.

causes.cause: Focal demyelination from ageing-related arterial tortuosity
causes.description: With age, intracranial arteries elongate and become tortuous, bringing the superior cerebellar artery into contact with the trigeminal nerve root. This explains the strong age association and the higher prevalence of classical TN in older adults.

A minority of TN patients have typical clinical features but no identifiable neurovascular compression on high-resolution MRI. Possible mechanisms include microvascular compression below MRI resolution, congenital anatomical predisposition, and subclinical demyelination from ageing.

riskFactors.description: Arterial tortuosity increases with age; the incidence of TN rises steeply in the sixth and seventh decades. Under age 40, secondary causes (MS, tumour) should be actively excluded.
Women are affected roughly 1.5-2× more frequently than men; the reason is unclear but hormonal influences on myelination and vascular anatomy have been proposed.
TN occurs in approximately 2% of MS patients — 20× the general population risk. The mechanism is demyelinating plaque at the trigeminal entry zone.

Hypertension accelerates arterial tortuosity and may increase the likelihood of neurovascular compression at the trigeminal root entry zone. Blood pressure control does not reliably reverse TN but is part of overall management.

Familial cases exist and suggest a possible genetic contribution to vascular anatomy or myelination susceptibility. First-degree relatives have a modestly elevated risk, though the absolute familial incidence is low.
A smaller posterior fossa with reduced cerebrospinal fluid cushion around the neurovascular structures may predispose to contact-induced compression even with non-tortuous vessels.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 50 account for the vast majority of TN cases; the condition is rare below age 40 except in the context of multiple sclerosis.
Women are diagnosed with TN at roughly 1.5-2× the rate of men; the right side of the face is affected slightly more often than the left in classical TN.
Multiple sclerosis patients have a 20-fold elevated risk of TN compared with the general population; TN may be the presenting symptom of MS in younger patients.
Geographic and ethnic variation is modest; TN occurs worldwide across all ethnic groups, but access to MVD surgery is strongly influenced by socioeconomic and healthcare-system factors.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia is primarily clinical, based on the International Classification of Headache Disorders (ICHD-3) criteria: unilateral brief paroxysmal pain in trigeminal territory, triggered by innocuous stimuli, and with the characteristic electric-shock quality. The clinical diagnosis is confirmed by a therapeutic response to carbamazepine — improvement in 70-80% of patients within 72 hours of an adequate dose strongly supports TN. MRI of the posterior fossa and brainstem is mandatory at diagnosis to exclude secondary causes (MS plaques, tumours, vascular malformations) and to identify neurovascular compression for surgical planning. High-resolution 3D MRI sequences — FIESTA or CISS — provide the best visualisation of the relationship between the trigeminal nerve and adjacent vessels at the root entry zone. Dental evaluation is critical to exclude dental pathology, as misdiagnosis as toothache causes an average diagnostic delay of 3 years and unnecessary extractions. Neurophysiological testing (trigeminal reflex studies, blink reflex) is useful when the clinical presentation is atypical or when objective evidence of nerve dysfunction is sought. Measurement of the carbamazepine blood level confirms therapeutic dosing when response is incomplete. Neuroimaging must be re-evaluated if pain changes character, becomes bilateral, or is accompanied by sensory loss not explained by prior ablative surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies neurovascular compression at the trigeminal root entry zone; excludes MS plaques, tumours, and vascular malformations
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the MRI scanner without contrast. Thin-slice 3D sequences through the posterior fossa show the artery or vein in contact with the trigeminal nerve. Required before any surgical procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnostic and therapeutic: an 80% or greater pain response to carbamazepine 200-400 mg/day strongly supports the TN diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A prescription for carbamazepine is started at 100-200 mg twice daily and titrated over 5-7 days. Pain relief within 72 hours strongly supports the diagnosis. Blood count and sodium are monitored due to rare haematological and electrolyte side effects.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes dental pathology (cracked tooth, pulpitis, abscess) that can mimic TN and has caused unnecessary extractions in up to 30% of patients before TN diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard dental examination with X-rays. If teeth and gums are normal and pain persists, TN becomes the likely diagnosis and neurology referral is appropriate.

diagnosticTests.test: Trigeminal reflex testing (blink reflex, masseter reflex)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects objective evidence of trigeminal nerve dysfunction supporting secondary TN or guiding ablative procedure planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Surface electrodes placed on the face record the reflex response to brief electrical stimulation near the supraorbital nerve. Takes 20-30 minutes; mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.test: CT brain with posterior fossa views
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid exclusion of mass lesion or calcified structure when MRI is contraindicated; less sensitive than MRI for neurovascular anatomy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard CT requiring no preparation; 5-10 minutes. Inferior to MRI for soft-tissue detail but useful in emergency or MRI-contraindicated settings.

treatmentOverview: First-line treatment is pharmacological: carbamazepine (200-1,200 mg/day) is effective in 70-80% of newly diagnosed patients and is endorsed by the European Federation of Neurological Societies (EFNS) guidelines. Oxcarbazepine (600-1,800 mg/day) has equivalent efficacy with a better tolerability profile. Add-on agents — lamotrigine, baclofen, gabapentin, pregabalin — augment pain control when monotherapy is insufficient. Drug efficacy wanes in many patients over time, and roughly 50% of patients with TN on long-term carbamazepine eventually require surgery for inadequate control or intolerable side effects (hyponatraemia, bone marrow suppression, cognitive blunting, diplopia). Three surgical approaches are available, each with distinct risk-benefit profiles. Microvascular decompression (MVD) via posterior fossa craniotomy is the most definitive treatment, addressing the underlying neurovascular compression; initial pain relief in 90% of patients with 70% free of pain medication at 5 years and 64% at 10 years (Barker et al. NEJM 1996). It carries the highest procedural risk — posterior fossa craniotomy — but the lowest risk of facial numbness. Percutaneous rhizotomy procedures — balloon compression, glycerol injection, or radiofrequency thermocoagulation — access the trigeminal ganglion through the foramen ovale with a needle; immediate pain relief exceeds 90% but recurrence rates are 25-50% at 5 years, and facial numbness is a near-universal trade-off. Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife) delivers a focused radiation dose to the trigeminal nerve root entry zone; pain relief develops over weeks to months in 70-80% of patients, with 50-55% free of medication at 3 years, making it ideal for older patients or those with significant medical comorbidity. The AANS and the European Association of Neurosurgical Societies (EANS) recommend MVD as the procedure of choice for medically fit patients with confirmed neurovascular compression.

medicalTreatments.name: Carbamazepine (200-1,200 mg/day in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacotherapy; a sodium-channel blocker that reduces ectopic trigeminal nerve firing. Start at 100-200 mg twice daily and titrate to the minimum effective dose. Monitor FBC, LFTs, and serum sodium. HLA-B*1502 testing before starting in patients of South and Southeast Asian descent to screen for Stevens-Johnson syndrome risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in 70-80% of newly diagnosed TN; efficacy declines in 30-50% of patients over time as disease progresses

medicalTreatments.name: Oxcarbazepine (600-1,800 mg/day in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line or alternative first-line; fewer drug interactions and lower bone marrow toxicity than carbamazepine but similar risk of hyponatraemia. Preferred in elderly patients and those on polypharmacy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to carbamazepine; slightly better tolerability profile; 60-80% initial pain control

medicalTreatments.name: Lamotrigine (add-on, 200-400 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Sodium and calcium channel blocker used as add-on when carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine alone is insufficient. Slow titration required to minimise rash risk (including Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50-60% additional pain reduction as add-on therapy in carbamazepine-partial responders

medicalTreatments.name: Baclofen (add-on, 30-80 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: GABA-B agonist that reduces trigeminal nucleus excitability. Used as add-on or when sodium-channel blockers are contraindicated. Dose limited by sedation, muscle weakness, and risk of withdrawal seizures on abrupt discontinuation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit in 50-60% of TN patients as add-on; weaker evidence base than carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine

medicalTreatments.name: Pregabalin (150-600 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-2-delta calcium channel ligand; reduces neuropathic pain and background aching in Type 2 TN with concomitant continuous pain. More evidence in neuropathic pain generally than TN specifically.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Useful for the continuous pain component of Type 2 TN; 30-40% additional pain reduction over sodium-channel blockers

surgicalOptions.name: Microvascular decompression (MVD — Jannetta procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: A posterior fossa craniotomy (4 cm keyhole incision behind the ear) exposes the trigeminal nerve root entry zone under the operating microscope. The offending artery or vein is separated from the nerve with a Teflon sponge. The most definitive surgery as it addresses the underlying cause and preserves trigeminal function.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial pain relief in 90%; pain-free without medication at 5 years in 70%, at 10 years in 64% (Barker et al. NEJM 1996); hearing loss or facial nerve injury under 1% at expert centres

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous balloon compression (Mullan procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: A trocar passes through the foramen ovale to place a Fogarty balloon catheter in Meckel's cave. Balloon inflation compresses the trigeminal ganglion for 1-2 minutes, creating selective injury to large myelinated fibres. Performed under general anaesthesia in 30-45 minutes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate pain relief over 90%; 5-year pain-free rate 50-60%; numbness in the V2-V3 distribution in virtually all patients; preferred for V1-distribution pain as corneal anaesthesia risk is lower than with RF thermocoagulation

surgicalOptions.name: Radiofrequency thermocoagulation (RF rhizotomy)
surgicalOptions.description: A needle passes through the foramen ovale to the trigeminal ganglion under fluoroscopic guidance. An electrode is positioned using electrical stimulation to provoke and map pain; a controlled thermal lesion is then made at 60-80°C. Performed under brief general anaesthesia with intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate pain relief over 90%; 5-year pain control in 50-60%; numbness in the treated division in 80-90%; risk of anaesthesia dolorosa (painful numbness) approximately 4%; risk of corneal anaesthesia when targeting V1

surgicalOptions.name: Glycerol rhizolysis
surgicalOptions.description: Anhydrous glycerol is injected through a needle into the trigeminal cistern (Meckel's cave) via the foramen ovale. The mechanism is direct neurotoxic injury. Performed under local anaesthesia, making it suitable for very elderly or medically frail patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate pain relief in 80-90%; 5-year pain control in 40-55%; less precise than RF thermocoagulation; numbness occurs in 50-60% of patients

surgicalOptions.name: Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife or CyberKnife)
surgicalOptions.description: A focal radiation dose of 70-90 Gy is delivered to the trigeminal nerve root entry zone, creating a controlled lesion that interrupts ectopic firing. Non-invasive and performed as a day procedure; ideal for older, medically frail patients and those who decline craniotomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial pain relief 70-80%; 50-55% pain-free off medication at 3 years; response latency 4-8 weeks; numbness in 25-35%; cannot be used in MS-related TN (risk of brainstem radiation injury)

alternativeTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin A injection into trigger zones
alternativeTreatments.description: Intradermal or subcutaneous injection of onabotulinumtoxinA (25-100 units) into the painful facial region reduces attack frequency and intensity by blocking peripheral pain transmitter release. Several randomised trials show benefit over placebo; reserved for patients who have failed pharmacotherapy and are not yet surgical candidates.

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcranial magnetic stimulation (repetitive rTMS)
alternativeTreatments.description: Non-invasive cortical stimulation of the motor cortex or prefrontal regions modulates pain processing. Limited to specialised centres; results are promising in small trials as bridge therapy while awaiting surgery.

whySeeSpecialist: A neurosurgeon experienced in posterior fossa surgery and facial pain should evaluate any patient with TN that fails carbamazepine at an adequate dose or whose side effects are intolerable. MVD should be considered in medically fit patients under 80 with confirmed neurovascular compression on MRI before multiple ablative procedures erode trigeminal function. Referral should not be delayed for years of dose escalation if quality of life is severely impaired.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a neurosurgeon who performs more than 25-30 MVD procedures per year, as outcome data consistently show lower complication rates and higher pain-free rates at high-volume centres. Confirm the surgeon has access to intraoperative brainstem auditory evoked potential monitoring and facial nerve monitoring to protect cranial nerves VII and VIII during the posterior fossa approach. For patients unfit for craniotomy, ensure the centre offers all three percutaneous options and radiosurgery so the most appropriate procedure can be selected.
hospitalCriteria: Experienced posterior fossa neurosurgical team performing at least 25-30 MVD operations per year
Intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring (BAEP, facial EMG) for MVD
Stereotactic radiosurgery unit (Gamma Knife or CyberKnife) for non-surgical candidates
High-resolution 3D MRI (FIESTA or CISS) for pre-operative neurovascular mapping
Dedicated facial pain multidisciplinary team including neurology, neurosurgery, and pain management
keyFacilities: Academic neurosurgery centres with dedicated cranial nerve and facial pain programmes
Centres offering all four surgical modalities (MVD, RF, balloon, radiosurgery) to individualise treatment
Gamma Knife radiosurgery centres for older or medically frail patients
preventionStrategies: There are no established modifiable risk factors that prevent TN onset; disease management focuses on prompt diagnosis to avoid years of unnecessary dental procedures.
Control hypertension to slow arterial tortuosity, which may reduce the risk of neurovascular compression progression.
In MS patients, optimise disease-modifying therapy to reduce frequency of demyelinating relapses that can trigger or worsen TN.
Avoid delaying surgical referral once pharmacological failure is recognised — prolonged untreated pain is associated with central sensitisation and worse post-surgical outcomes.
lifestyleModifications: Identify and minimise personal trigger activities (specific foods, cold air, touching particular facial areas) to reduce attack frequency during medical management.
Maintain nutrition through soft, room-temperature foods and liquid supplements during high-attack frequency periods when normal eating is impossible.
Address depression and anxiety with psychological support or antidepressants — comorbid depression affects up to 40% of TN patients and impairs quality of life independently.
Avoid abrupt carbamazepine discontinuation — withdrawal can precipitate severe attack clusters and, rarely, seizures.
After surgery, protect the numb facial area from injury (eye protection if corneal numbness is present) and attend follow-up for pain outcome assessment.

recommended: Soft, lukewarm foods (smoothies, yogurt, soup, soft bread) that minimise jaw movement and trigger activation during painful periods
Nutritional supplements or meal-replacement shakes when chewing is severely impaired and caloric intake is inadequate
Adequate fluid intake to prevent dehydration, especially if eating is significantly restricted
Anti-inflammatory diet (oily fish, olive oil, berries) to support overall neural health, though no direct TN evidence
avoid: Hard, crunchy, or very hot or cold foods that reliably trigger attacks in individual patients
Alcohol, which can worsen carbamazepine side effects (sedation, ataxia)
Excess caffeine, which may lower seizure and pain thresholds
Food requiring prolonged vigorous chewing when attacks are frequent

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise (walking, swimming in warm water, yoga) is safe and beneficial for mood, sleep, and general health in TN patients. Avoid activities that expose the face to strong cold wind or require sustained jaw clenching, which can trigger attacks. After MVD, patients are typically advised to avoid strenuous physical activity for 6-8 weeks and to protect the posterior fossa incision from trauma.
dailyManagement: Take carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine at consistent intervals with food to maintain steady drug levels and reduce side effects.
Keep a pain diary recording attack frequency, duration, severity, and triggers to guide medication titration and document surgical outcomes.
Maintain dental and oral hygiene carefully using a soft brush and being mindful of trigger zones, as oral hygiene neglect from pain avoidance increases dental disease risk.
Carry a medication card noting the TN diagnosis and drug regime in case emergency dental treatment is needed — dental anaesthetics work normally in TN.
Monitor serum sodium (carbamazepine causes hyponatraemia) and blood count every 3-6 months during long-term therapy.
Schedule regular outpatient reviews with a neurologist or neurosurgeon to reassess surgical candidacy as drug efficacy wanes over time.
prognosis: Trigeminal neuralgia is a chronic condition with a fluctuating course: approximately 50% of patients have spontaneous remissions lasting months to years before relapsing. Pharmacological treatment controls pain in 70-80% of newly diagnosed patients, but efficacy declines in half of patients within 5-10 years due to disease progression and drug tolerance. MVD, the most definitive surgical treatment, achieves pain-free status without medication in approximately 64% of patients at 10 years. Percutaneous procedures achieve similar early results but have 25-50% recurrence at 5 years and progressively increase trigeminal sensory loss. Radiosurgery offers 50-55% sustained pain freedom at 3 years. Repeated ablative procedures reduce the chance of successful pain control and increase the risk of anaesthesia dolorosa — paradoxical painful numbness that is more disabling than TN itself and has no reliable treatment. Depression, anxiety, and social isolation are major secondary morbidities; their treatment substantially improves quality of life independently of pain control.
recoveryTimeline: MVD: hospital stay 2-4 days, return to light activity at 4-6 weeks, full activity at 8-12 weeks; facial pain relief is typically immediate. Percutaneous rhizotomy: day procedure, return to normal activity within days; numbness develops over hours and is permanent. Radiosurgery: day procedure with no immediate recovery; pain relief develops over 4-8 weeks.
complications: Anaesthesia dolorosa — a permanent, severe, continuous burning or aching pain in the numb facial region, occurring in approximately 1-4% after ablative procedures; has no reliable treatment and is more disabling than the original TN.

Corneal anaesthesia — loss of corneal protective sensation after ablative targeting of V1, risking keratitis, corneal ulceration, and visual loss; preventable by limiting treatment to V2/V3 distributions or using balloon compression.
Hearing loss or tinnitus in 1-2% of MVD cases from manipulation near the cochlear nerve or brainstem; largely preventable with intraoperative BAEP monitoring.
Posterior fossa haematoma or cerebellar injury: rare (under 1%) but serious complication of MVD requiring re-exploration.
Radiation-induced brainstem injury after radiosurgery: rare but severe; risk is higher with repeated radiosurgery or in patients with MS who have reduced brainstem reserve.
Recurrence of pain after initial successful surgery: 15-25% after MVD at 10 years; 30-50% after ablative procedures at 5 years; each recurrence is more difficult to treat than the first.

supportResources.name: Facial Pain Association (TNA — Trigeminal Neuralgia Association)
supportResources.description: The leading US patient organisation for TN; offers treatment centre directory, support groups, and educational materials for patients and families.

supportResources.name: American Association of Neurological Surgeons — Trigeminal Neuralgia
supportResources.description: Patient-facing surgical treatment overview from the AANS, including MVD, percutaneous procedures, and radiosurgery comparisons.

supportResources.name: The Trigeminal Neuralgia Association UK (TNA UK)
supportResources.description: UK-based support charity providing patient guides, helpline, and specialist centre referral information.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia (Medical Management)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: This page covers surgical management. Medical management with carbamazepine is first-line and appropriate for newly diagnosed or drug-responsive patients. Surgical options are reserved for pharmacological failure, intolerable side effects, or quality-of-life impact warranting intervention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neuropathic Facial Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Persistent idiopathic facial pain and atypical facial pain produce continuous burning or aching without the stereotyped electric-shock paroxysms and trigger zones of TN. They do not respond to carbamazepine or to neurosurgical decompression, and require neuropathic pain protocols, psychological support, and sometimes antidepressants.

confusedWithConditions.name: Central Pain Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Central pain syndrome, often post-stroke, produces constant rather than paroxysmal facial or body pain with allodynia. Unlike TN, there is no discrete trigger, no complete pain-free interval, and no response to carbamazepine. Imaging shows a central lesion rather than a peripheral nerve compression.

relatedConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Medical management of TN precedes surgical consideration; carbamazepine is the proven first-line treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: The pain of TN is neuropathic in mechanism; drugs used for other neuropathic conditions (gabapentin, pregabalin) are used as adjuncts.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: MS is the most important secondary cause of TN and explains early-onset or bilateral cases.

relatedConditions.name: Central Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-procedure anaesthesia dolorosa and MS-related central pain are part of the differential and management challenge in TN.

faqs.question: What is the best surgery for trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Microvascular decompression (MVD) is the most effective and durable surgery, with 64% of patients pain-free without medication at 10 years. It is the first choice for medically fit patients with confirmed neurovascular compression on MRI. Radiosurgery or percutaneous rhizotomy are alternatives for older or medically frail patients.

faqs.question: What is microvascular decompression (MVD)?
faqs.answer: MVD is a posterior fossa craniotomy performed under general anaesthesia. The neurosurgeon moves the offending artery off the trigeminal nerve root and places a Teflon sponge between them. The operation takes 2-4 hours; hospital stay is 2-4 days. About 90% of patients have immediate pain relief.

faqs.question: What is the success rate of trigeminal neuralgia surgery?
faqs.answer: MVD provides initial pain relief in 90% of patients, with 70% pain-free without medication at 5 years and 64% at 10 years. Percutaneous rhizotomy achieves 90% initial relief but 25-50% recurrence at 5 years. Radiosurgery achieves 70-80% initial relief and 50-55% medication-free pain control at 3 years.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after MVD surgery?
faqs.answer: Hospital stay after MVD is 2-4 days. Most patients return to light activities in 4-6 weeks and full activity in 8-12 weeks. Facial pain relief is typically immediate on waking from anaesthesia. Headache from the posterior fossa approach is common for 1-2 weeks.

faqs.question: What is Gamma Knife radiosurgery for trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Gamma Knife radiosurgery delivers a focused 70-90 Gy radiation dose to the trigeminal nerve root entry zone in a single outpatient session. Pain relief develops over 4-8 weeks in 70-80% of patients. It is non-invasive and suitable for elderly patients or those with significant medical comorbidity.

faqs.question: What happens during percutaneous rhizotomy?
faqs.answer: A needle is passed through the cheek and skull base foramen (foramen ovale) to reach the trigeminal ganglion. Depending on the technique, a balloon is inflated, radiofrequency heat is applied, or glycerol is injected to damage pain fibres. It is a day procedure performed under brief anaesthesia.

faqs.question: Does trigeminal neuralgia surgery cause facial numbness?
faqs.answer: MVD usually preserves sensation. Percutaneous ablative procedures (radiofrequency, balloon, glycerol) produce facial numbness in 50-90% of patients — this is the mechanism of pain relief. Radiosurgery causes numbness in 25-35%. Numbness is generally accepted as a trade-off for pain control.

faqs.question: Can trigeminal neuralgia recur after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence rates vary: MVD has 15-25% recurrence at 10 years. Percutaneous rhizotomy recurs in 30-50% at 5 years. Radiosurgery has 40-50% recurrence at 5 years. Repeat procedures are possible but each successive surgery carries higher risk of permanent sensory complications.

faqs.question: Is trigeminal neuralgia surgery safe for elderly patients?
faqs.answer: Percutaneous procedures (balloon, glycerol, radiofrequency) and radiosurgery are low-risk day procedures well tolerated in elderly patients with comorbidities. MVD via craniotomy carries higher anaesthetic risk in frail patients over 80; the decision requires individualised anaesthetic assessment.

faqs.question: What is anaesthesia dolorosa?
faqs.answer: Anaesthesia dolorosa is a rare but severe complication of ablative trigeminal procedures: paradoxical burning or aching pain in the numb facial region, which can be more disabling than the original trigeminal neuralgia. It occurs in approximately 1-4% of ablative cases and has no reliable treatment.

faqs.question: Why is carbamazepine tried before surgery?
faqs.answer: Carbamazepine controls pain in 70-80% of newly diagnosed TN patients and is first-line per EFNS guidelines. Surgery is reserved for pharmacological failure, intolerable side effects, or patient preference. Most guidelines recommend an adequate trial of at least two drugs before surgical referral.

faqs.question: Can trigeminal neuralgia be caused by a tumour?
faqs.answer: Yes, rarely. Secondary TN from posterior fossa tumours (meningioma, epidermoid cyst, acoustic neuroma) accounts for 5-8% of cases. MRI is required at diagnosis to exclude this. Tumour-related TN requires removal or treatment of the underlying lesion as the primary surgical goal.

faqs.question: What is the difference between MVD and percutaneous rhizotomy?
faqs.answer: MVD addresses the cause (nerve compression) without damaging the nerve, preserving sensation with durable results. Percutaneous rhizotomy deliberately injures the trigeminal nerve to block pain signals, producing numbness. MVD offers longer pain freedom but requires a full craniotomy under general anaesthesia.

faqs.question: Does multiple sclerosis affect trigeminal neuralgia surgery outcomes?
faqs.answer: Yes. MS-related TN has worse surgical outcomes than classical TN. MVD success rates fall to 50-60% when no identifiable vascular compression is found. Radiosurgery carries a risk of brainstem radiation injury in MS patients. Percutaneous procedures are generally preferred for MS-related TN.

faqs.question: How do I find a surgeon for trigeminal neuralgia?
faqs.answer: Seek a neurosurgeon performing more than 25-30 MVD operations per year at a centre with intraoperative brainstem monitoring. The Facial Pain Association directory and academic medical centres with dedicated cranial nerve or facial pain programmes are the best starting points. Confirm the surgeon offers all surgical modalities.

metaTitle: Trigeminal Neuralgia Surgery: MVD & Outcomes | aihealz
metaDescription: Trigeminal neuralgia surgery: MVD achieves 64% pain freedom at 10 years. Radiosurgery and rhizotomy options, risks, and recovery timelines explained here.
